fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this Bitcoin chain-state and transaction indexing toolkit we've been talking about. The core idea is that developers should be able to plug in their block/transaction observations and get back clean, queryable results without having to write all that painful reconciliation code themselves. Think of it like the wallet-sync plumbing we did for the Lightning stuff last quarter — same philosophy, just applied to UTXO tracking and chain state.

The main things we need covered: handling chain tip updates including when blocks get reorganized out, inserting individual checkpoints, figuring out how much value is flowing in/out for scripts we care about, tracking whether a script has been 'used' yet, computing fees, resolving conflicts when we have both a loose output record and a full transaction for the same coin, tracking which tx spent which output, deriving scripts in a lookahead window, and finally filtering down to just the canonical transaction history when there are conflicting spends — confirmed vs mempool, newer vs older, that kind of thing.

One important thing: the I/O adapter layer that reads commands and writes results should stay completely separate from the actual business logic — we burned ourselves last time by mixing those together. Also please keep the file structure sensible, not everything crammed into one giant file. Language is Rust. Tests are in the rcb_tests folder if you need to cross-reference expected behavior.

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in. On chain updates, if an update can’t be connected because we’re missing the checkpoints needed in between, the output needs to be exactly two lines: `error=cannot_connect` and then `try_include_height=<N>`, where N is the lowest height that has to exist to make that update connectable. So if somebody sends a disjoint update starting at height 3 while the chain is empty, that should come back as `try_include_height=0`.

For reorgs, the changeset needs to show both sides of what happened, not just what we ended up with. Removed checkpoints should be emitted as `<height>:<removed>` and that literal `<removed>` string is the hash placeholder. Anything new or replacing something shows up as `<height>:<hash>`. That whole list is comma-separated inside square brackets and ordered by height ascending. Then the final `chain=` line should only show the checkpoints that actually survive after the removal. Also related here, if an update happens to agree with the existing chain at two different points, like both chains sharing genesis and tip, the changeset should only include the actually newly added checkpoints. The final chain still includes ALL checkpoints, meaning the old ones that were already there plus the new ones, sorted by height, and matching checkpoints that already existed should not get repeated in the changeset.

On single checkpoint insert, if we try to insert at a height that already exists but the hash is different, we should not mutate anything at all. The output there needs to be `error=conflicting_block`, `height=<N>`, `original_hash=<existing_hash>`, `update_hash=<attempted_hash>`, and `chain=[<unchanged>]`. Chain state stays exactly as it was.

For canonical conflict handling, the priority order is important. A confirmed transaction on the active chain always beats any conflicting unconfirmed transaction. If both conflicting transactions are unconfirmed, the more recently-seen one wins. The one extra wrinkle is that if a transaction has unconfirmed descendants, that whole branch is treated as canonical even if that parent is older than the conflicting transaction that has no descendants. Anything non-canonical, plus its descendants, should be left out of `chain_txs`, `chain_txouts`, `unspents`, and the balance math.

Balance reporting also needs to stay split into the four buckets we discussed: `balance_immature` for coinbase outputs not yet matured, `balance_trusted_pending` for unconfirmed outputs from transactions we sent ourselves, `balance_untrusted_pending` for unconfirmed outputs from external unconfirmed transactions, and `balance_confirmed` for outputs in confirmed blocks. Only canonical unspent outputs should count toward any of those buckets.

On next unused script selection, we should prefer filling the lowest unused gap before deriving anything new. So if 0-15 are used, 16 is unused, and 17-26 are used, `next_unused` should return `index=16` with `is_new=false` and `changed=0`. We only derive a brand new script once every previously revealed index is already used, and that’s when it should return `is_new=true` and `changed=1`.

And just to restate the architecture part because a few people asked what “separate” really means here: the execution adapter in `src/adapter/` (or equivalent) should be the layer that turns JSON/string commands into normal Rust calls into the core domain, with stdin/stdout parsing kept completely out of the business logic in `src/domain/` or `src/core/`. Same idea for dependencies in general: the high-level chain/tx pieces should depend on abstractions (traits), not concrete I/O implementations, in the same spirit as BDK’s existing chain-source abstraction.

trigger_keywords: script value sent received net calculation
how is sent calculated for a spending transaction
value accounting for tracked scripts
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For script value accounting: 'received' is the sum of all outputs in the transaction that pay to a tracked script. 'sent' is the sum of all previously-scanned tracked outputs that this transaction spends as inputs. 'net' is received minus sent. A transaction that only receives (no tracked inputs) has sent=0. A transaction spending a previously-scanned tracked output counts that output's full value as sent, even if the transaction also pays back to a tracked script.

trigger_keywords: script usage unmark with scanned output
can you unmark a script that has an indexed outpoint
scanned output prevents unused
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A script that has been made 'used' by a scanned/indexed output cannot be returned to 'unused' state via manual unmark. Attempting to unmark such a script returns changed=false and the script remains used. The 'indexed_outpoints' count reflects how many on-chain outputs are associated with the script. Manual mark/unmark only works freely when no scanned output has touched the script.

trigger_keywords: negative fee error format
missing previous output error
coinbase fee result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Fee calculation has three distinct outcomes: (1) valid fee outputs 'fee=<satoshis>'; (2) if known inputs sum to less than outputs, output 'error=negative_fee' then 'amount=<negative_number>' (the signed difference); (3) if any input references an unknown previous output, output 'error=missing_previous_output' then 'missing_count=<N>'. Coinbase transactions (null/empty inputs) produce fee=0 and no error.

trigger_keywords: floating output vs full transaction precedence
which record wins when there is a conflict
full transaction authoritative over floating
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the same outpoint is described by both a floating output record and a full transaction record, the full transaction record is always authoritative regardless of insertion order. If the full transaction does not have a vout1 but a floating record claims it exists, vout1 is absent. The reported value for vout0 must always match what the full transaction says, never the floating record's value.

trigger_keywords: coinbase spend edge
null outpoint spend tracking
coinbase input should not create spender
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Coinbase inputs (null previous outpoints, i.e. all-zeros txid with index 0xFFFFFFFF) must NOT be recorded as spend edges in the spend graph. Querying spenders of the null outpoint must return 0. Only non-coinbase inputs create spend edges. Spend edges are insertion-order independent: whether the spending transaction is inserted before or after the funding transaction, the spender count must be identical.

trigger_keywords: lookahead scan limit behavior
when does scanning extend the revealed window
scan index beyond lookahead does not extend
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Lookahead scanning extends the revealed window only when the scanned index falls within the current lookahead range (i.e., index <= last_revealed + lookahead). If the scanned index is beyond that range, the window does not extend and changed=0. The lookahead is a fixed constant (10 in the test cases). Scanning index 41 when last_revealed=30 and lookahead=10 produces changed=0 and leaves last_revealed=30.

trigger_keywords: non wildcard descriptor script count
fixed single script template reveal behavior
reveal to 200 on non-wildcard descriptor
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Non-wildcard (fixed) descriptors produce exactly one script total and never derive additional scripts regardless of how high a target reveal index is requested. Calling reveal_to_target(200) on a non-wildcard descriptor returns changed_count=0 and the total script count remains 1. next_unused returns index=0 with is_new=false once the single script has been revealed. Attempting a second reveal returns 0 changes.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: bdk raw string command stdin
not json action
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The public adapter input is a raw string command from stdin, such as local_chain_update:add_first_tip, fee:positive_fee, derivation:set_targets, or conflict:newer_unconfirmed_wins. Do not require JSON or a top-level action field for these cases; parse the command group and scenario from the raw input string and emit the exact expected stdout.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Tests are in the rcb_tests folder if you need to cross-reference expected behavior
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ (feature1 through feature9) each define the exact string format of adapter-layer outputs; these are the black-box contracts for the execution adapter, not for the core domain model itself

context_pointers.vague_reference: keep the file structure sensible, not everything crammed into one giant file
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Architecture NFR #1 in start.md: the project MUST use a multi-file directory structure (src/, tests/, etc.) with distinct modules for parsing, routing, validation, core execution, and output formatting — a single-file solution is explicitly prohibited for this domain complexity

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that constraint in the current requirements. Please check the rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ directory for the relevant feature's JSON file, which defines the exact expected output format as the authoritative black-box contract. If you need clarification on business intent rather than format, feel free to rephrase your question.

feature1_local_chain_updates.json.description: Apply checkpoint-chain updates to an existing chain, reporting either the exact applied checkpoint changes and final chain or a normalized connection error.

feature2_local_chain_insertions.json.description: Insert one checkpoint into a local chain, preserving the chain on conflicts and reporting the exact accepted change or normalized conflict details.

feature3_script_value_accounting.json.description: Track registered locking scripts and report sent, received, and net value for transactions before and after scanning prior outputs.

feature4_script_usage_state.json.description: Maintain whether tracked locking scripts are unused, manually used, or made permanently used by scanned outputs.

feature5_transaction_fee_calculation.jso.description: Calculate transaction fee from known previous outputs and normalize negative-fee or missing-input conditions.

feature6_output_precedence.json.description: Resolve conflicts between floating output records and full transaction records by giving the full transaction output authoritative value.

feature7_spend_tracking.json.description: Track transaction spends independently from insertion order while ignoring coinbase null inputs as spend edges.

feature8_script_derivation.json.description: Reveal and scan deterministic script derivations with target indices, lookahead windows, unused-script selection, and non-wildcard descriptors.
